summary.intent: Fix a series of bugs in the Gossamer VS Code extension: server port conflicts, missing database schema, checkpoints not showing in the treeview, multi-repo DB architecture, and several UI/UX issues.
summary.outcome: Fixed all major issues: server orphan detection and cleanup, pushSchema comment-filter bug that silently dropped all SQL statements, per-repo DB registry replacing the single primary DB, gitlog maxBuffer overflow preventing checkpoint-to-commit mapping, session sorting by workspace, and several UI fixes including homebrew install command, MIT license, and git username display.

repo: Each repo has its own SQLite DB under ~/.gossamer/repos/<name>.db — there is no primary/secondary hierarchy, all DBs are equal peers.
Shadow branches are written by the Entire CLI (not Gossamer) under refs/heads/entire/<HEAD-short>-<hash>. Gossamer only indexes what Entire writes.
Checkpoint commits live on entire/checkpoints/v1 branch with 'Checkpoint:' in the subject; feature branch commits get 'Entire-Checkpoint:' trailers in the body.
The dbCache in packages/core/src/db.ts caches Prisma clients by path — a corrupt/empty DB that gets deleted still returns the broken cached client unless evictDb() is called.

The gitlog npm package defaults to nameStatus: true (adds --name-status to git log), which causes stdout maxBuffer overflow on large repos with many commits, silently killing checkpoint-to-gitOid mapping.
macOS com.apple.provenance extended attribute can block SQLite I/O, causing 'disk I/O error' that masquerades as a schema/table issue.
git worktree add fails with 'already used by worktree' when two repos try to check out the same branch — fixed with --detach flag.
The Entire CLI hooks (session-start, user-prompt-submit, stop, session-end) are configured per-project in .claude/settings.json, not globally.

code.finding: pushSchema was only called for the primary DB at startup, not for all configured repos in the config.
pushSchema split schema.sql on ';\n' then filtered chunks with !s.startsWith('--'), but every chunk in schema.sql starts with '-- CreateTable', so all 56 statements were silently discarded.
findCommitForCheckpoint used gitlog with nameStatus not disabled, causing maxBuffer overflow on large repos. Fixed by adding nameStatus: false and execOptions: { maxBuffer: 100MB }.
spawnServer guard only checked the in-memory GossamerPanel.server static variable — orphaned server processes from crashed VS Code sessions would hold the port, causing EADDRINUSE on next activation.
GET /api/v2/sessions/:id/checkpoints queried the single primary 'db' variable, returning empty for sessions in any other repo's DB.
GET /api/repos/current only matched against server startup repoDir, always returning null for other workspaces, causing spurious re-registration on every window activation.
None of the webview API calls passed ?localPath=, so all session/log/checkpoint requests had to probe all DBs sequentially instead of resolving directly.
EventItem was rendered without a user prop, so the human label was always hardcoded to 'You' even though session.gitUserName was available.
Homebrew tap command was 'entire/tap/entire' instead of 'entireio/tap/entire' — wrong tap org name.

workflow: When pushSchema silently does nothing, the SQLite file is still created (header only, 4096 bytes) giving a false impression that initialization succeeded.
Compiled dist/ can be outdated when investigating bugs — always rebuild before testing to ensure fixes are actually in play.
Use 'sqlite3 <db> .tables' to directly verify schema state rather than trusting application logs.
The gitlog npm package's maxBuffer issue surfaces as silent null returns from findCommitForCheckpoint, not as thrown errors, because the catch in saveGitOidMapping swallows everything.
Testing pushSchema in isolation (node --input-type=module) can succeed while the compiled server fails due to different code paths or caching.

friction: Multiple iterations on pushSchema fix due to not catching the comment-filter bug before shipping — the fix appeared correct in isolation but the root cause was different from what was diagnosed.
The 'adopting orphaned server' approach was shipped first but immediately caused 'error loading sessions' because the stale server served a different repo — required a second fix to kill-and-respawn.
Silent error swallowing in saveGitOidMapping (catch block with no logging) made the gitlog maxBuffer bug invisible for weeks.
macOS com.apple.provenance xattr on gossamer-test.db caused a 'disk I/O error' that looked identical to a schema problem, sending diagnosis down the wrong path.
The dbCache in getDb meant deleting a corrupt DB file wasn't enough — the cached broken Prisma client kept being returned, requiring evictDb() to be added and exported.
The Entire CLI stopped writing shadow branches and checkpoints ~3 weeks ago — no new session data available for testing live update flow.
open_items: Entire CLI is not writing new shadow branches or checkpoint commits for cosmos-graphql (~3 weeks of inactivity) — root cause unknown, likely the background watcher process died silently.
Live session updates for cosmos-graphql not verifiable because Entire CLI isn't producing new data — the Gossamer indexing chain was confirmed working but end-to-end update flow untested.
GET /api/sessions/:id probe only checks shadowSession table — sessions that exist only via checkpointSessionMetadata or logEvent may still not be found in some edge cases.
The restart() method in GossamerPanel doesn't wait for the old server to die before spawning a new one — potential race condition on rapid restarts.
Gossamer has no fallback to show sessions that exist as files on disk (.entire/metadata/) but haven't been committed to a shadow branch yet.
Only 9 of 17 expected gitOid mappings were created for cosmos-graphql after the maxBuffer fix — remaining 8 may exceed the 10,000 commit limit or have other issues.

The single 'db' variable removal from startServer() means serve.ts no longer passes dbPath — the startup schema push now relies entirely on the config file having all repos, which may miss the repo passed via --repo-dir if it isn't in config yet.
